 =20
   =20
      Insider trading
   =20
 =20

I guess it is time for an  Eleventh Law of Insider Trading, and frankly it=
=E2=80=99s a little weird=C2=A0that this has never come up before,=C2=A0but=
:=C2=A0Don=E2=80=99t ... insider trade =E2=80=A6=C2=A0while =E2=80=A6=C2=A0=
drunk?=C2=A0

A former Barclays Plc trader said he got an insider tip from an ex-Goldman =
Sachs Group Inc. vice president at a squash court after having too many dri=
nks.
That trade netted a $2,000 profit, just a fraction of the $280,000 the two =
men made in illicit profits from insider trades in 2017, according to Aksha=
y Niranjan. The former trader testified against his friend at an insider-tr=
ading trial in Manhattan Wednesday. =E2=80=A6
Under questioning from prosecutor Sam Rothschild, Niranjan told the jury th=
at he suspected Goel=E2=80=99s stock recommendations must have come from co=
nfidential information he received at Goldman. His concern grew when Goel t=
exted him in coded messages, and insisted on discussing trades only in pers=
on.
=E2=80=9CIt was great to make money,=E2=80=9D Niranjan said. =E2=80=9CAt th=
e same time, I was concerned. I told him I hope he=E2=80=99s not breaking t=
he law. He said, don=E2=80=99t worry, he knows the rules. He would not do s=
omething as stupid as that.=E2=80=9D
Niranjan said one day he got a text from Goel to play squash after work and=
 despite first going out for some drinks he showed up. It was there that Go=
el recommended buying options in Lumos Pharma Inc., a company Niranjan said=
 he never heard of.
The next day Goel texted him, =E2=80=9CDid you book the court?=E2=80=9D Nir=
anjan said he was confused, because there had been no plan to play squash. =
After it became clear to him that Goel wanted him to buy the options in Lum=
os, he bought $2,000 worth of call options. A few days later, the company a=
nnounced it was being acquired, the stock price went up, and the trade gene=
rated $2,000 in profit.

Don=E2=80=99t play squash while drunk either, probably, though you make you=
r own decisions on that one. I do love the idea that this guy got stock tip=
s from his buddy on the squash court and was like =E2=80=9Cahh these tips a=
re probably legit,=E2=80=9D and then his buddy made such a show of spycraft=
 and communicating in code that he was eventually like =E2=80=9Cwait a minu=
te, we are doing crimes here!=E2=80=9D The lesson is, =E2=80=9Cdon=E2=80=99=
t call bribes chickens=E2=80=9D: If you go around ostentatiously talking in=
 code, people might not know what crimes you are doing, but=C2=A0they will =
know you are doing crimes. If you go around like =E2=80=9CI really enjoy an=
alyzing stocks, and after reading message boards and researching the compan=
y I think that this Lumos Pharma company is going places,=E2=80=9D at least=
 your trial will be less embarrassing.

 =20
   =20
      AI vs. AI
   =20
 =20

A big modern theme in financial markets, or at least in the popular imagina=
tion of financial markets, is humans trying to trick robots. You see this a=
t the market-microstructure level: There are, in the world, people who   cl=
ick really fast in their brokerage accounts to try to spoof high-frequency-=
trading algorithms. (Navinder Singh Sarao, who was arrested for spoofing th=
at caused a flash crash in 2010, viewed himself as fighting =E2=80=9Cthe ma=
ss manipulation of the high frequency nerds.=E2=80=9D) And you see it in se=
curities fraud: People write   fake press releases and  fake Securities and=
 Exchange Commission filings about mergers, not so much because they expect=
=C2=A0other people=C2=A0to find those filings=C2=A0believable, but because =
they expect=C2=A0trading algorithms=C2=A0to see the headlines and react to =
them immediately =E2=80=94=C2=A0driving up the price of the stock and allow=
ing them to make a quick profit before anyone notices that the news is fake=
.
The next theme might be robots trying to trick robots? Generative artificia=
l intelligence =E2=80=9Cwill pile pressure on the labor force, especially f=
or higher-wage knowledge workers whose activities =E2=80=98were previously =
considered to be relatively immune from automation,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D   say=
s a McKinsey report; it would be funny if McKinsey had a large language mod=
el write the report.
  [1]
=C2=A0Surely one type of knowledge work that would be particularly suscepti=
ble to AI automation would be the business of tricking robots? The robots, =
after all, know how to think like robots.
  [2]

And so at the market-microstructure level, it is easy to imagine letting an=
 artificial intelligence model loose on the stock market=C2=A0and telling i=
t =E2=80=9Clearn how to trade profitably,=E2=80=9D and the model coming bac=
k and saying =E2=80=9Cit seems like the stock market is dominated by simple=
 market-making algorithms that respond to order-book information, and actua=
lly the way to trade profitably is to do a lot of spoofing and market manip=
ulation to trick them.=E2=80=9D (This is more or less a plot point in Andre=
w Lipstein=E2=80=99s upcoming hedge-fund novel=C2=A0The Vegan.)
And at the level of writing fake press releases, generative AI is probably =
better at writing fake press releases (and illustrating them with convincin=
g fake photos) than, you know, the average market manipulator is. The  Fina=
ncial Times reports:

AI-generated news and images could pose a big problem for hedge funds and u=
ltrafast proprietary trading firms that use complex algorithms to comb vast=
 amounts of news and social media for market-moving signals that they can t=
hen rapidly trade.
While their computers have become more adept at sifting out false news stor=
ies and social media posts, executives at quantitative trading firms warn t=
hat machine-generated misinformation is a new frontier.
=E2=80=9CAI, to be sure, opens the door to all manner of mischief in the in=
formation environment, which is becoming harder to manage,=E2=80=9D said Do=
ug Greenig, founder of hedge fund Florin Court Capital, which bets on longe=
r-term trends in alternative markets rather than on very short-term market =
moves.
Traders=E2=80=99 particular concern is AI=E2=80=99s fast-developing ability=
 to produce highly convincing images and stories, and in huge quantities.
That could provide a huge number of pitfalls for proprietary trading firms =
and hedge funds that for years have been investing heavily in algorithms th=
at parse critical information, assess the language and sentiment within a s=
ource and use that data as a signal to trigger an automated trade. ...
Yin Luo, head of quantitative research at New York data group Wolfe Researc=
h, predicts =E2=80=9Ca cat-and-mouse game=E2=80=9D between parties spreadin=
g market-moving fake news and traders hoping to stay one step ahead.

It is funny to imagine an end state here in which markets are entirely domi=
nated by computers=C2=A0playing games with each other that are so complex a=
nd unintuitive that no human can understand them, so that a company will an=
nounce surprising good news and the stock will go down and everyone will be=
 like =E2=80=9Ceh, bots, what can you do.=E2=80=9D
Would it be=C2=A0so=C2=A0different from how financial markets work now? Fin=
ancial markets aggregate human views and preferences in an emergent way, so=
 that it=E2=80=99s not obvious what the views and preferences are just from=
 the price action. =E2=80=9CStocks went up because bad economic data convin=
ced investors that the Fed would pause its rate hikes=E2=80=9D is not reall=
y=C2=A0that=C2=A0much more intuitive or accurate an explanation than, like,=
 =E2=80=9Cstocks went up because some computers tricked some other computer=
s=C2=A0into thinking economic data was good.=E2=80=9D

 =20
   =20
      BlackRock Bitcoin ETF
   =20
 =20

One easily imaginable but also kind of weird future for crypto would be:
=09Bitcoin, and I guess Ethereum, are respectable and fully domesticated pa=
rts of the traditional financial system. Big asset managers, managing money=
 for pension funds or college endowments or insurance companies or your 401=
(k),=C2=A0buy billions of dollars of Bitcoin by calling up their sales cove=
rage at Goldman Sachs or JPMorgan, and then hold all those Bitcoin in stand=
ard respectable institutional custody arrangements.=09Pretty much every oth=
er cryptocurrency is banned in the US and eventually withers down to nothin=
g.

I do think this would be strange? Much of the activity by developers and in=
vestors and venture capitalists in crypto in recent years has been about, i=
n some way,=C2=A0improving on=C2=A0the ideas of Bitcoin (and Ethereum); it =
would be kind of weird for the base layer of crypto to be legal and mainstr=
eam and for everything built on top of it to be illegal. Also my general se=
nse of crypto ethos is that Bitcoin has traditionally been pretty correlate=
d with, like, =E2=80=9Canarchist online drug market,=E2=80=9D while more re=
cent crypto projects have been more like =E2=80=9Cventure capitalists, tech=
 founders and financial companies building new financial technology busines=
ses=E2=80=9D; it=E2=80=99s weird that the former would go mainstream and th=
e latter would be banned.
And yet it makes a certain sense: Bitcoin is=C2=A0not=C2=A0a fundraising to=
ol for building a new tech platform, so it=E2=80=99s not a=C2=A0security=C2=
=A0under US law, so the US Securities and Exchange Commission can=E2=80=99t=
 crack down on it. Everything else is in some loose sense =E2=80=9Cquasi-st=
ock in a quasi-company building a tech business,=E2=80=9D so selling or tra=
ding it risks violating US securities laws.
Anyway I=E2=80=99m not saying this is the most likely outcome, but the SEC =
definitely  seems to want Part 2=C2=A0(while grudgingly  tolerating Bitcoin=
 and Ethereum), and as for Part 1  here=E2=80=99s this:

BlackRock Inc. is trying its hand at potentially getting the first spot-Bit=
coin exchange-traded fund launched in the US.=C2=A0
The world=E2=80=99s largest asset manager on Thursday applied for the iShar=
es Bitcoin Trust, according to a filing with the US Securities and Exchange=
 Commission. Coinbase Global Inc., the biggest crypto exchange in the US, w=
ould act as custodian. The ETF, should it launch, would trade on Nasdaq.=C2=
=A0
This is at least the 33rd attempt by issuers for a spot-Bitcoin product, ac=
cording to a tally from Bloomberg Intelligence. But applications have faced=
 opposition by regulators, who have in the past cited market concerns and a=
 lack of investor protections, among other things.=C2=A0

I have no idea if the SEC will approve this, given that it didn=E2=80=99t a=
pprove the other 32, but on the other hand BlackRock is pretty smart and we=
ll-connected, and why would they try it if they didn=E2=80=99t think it wou=
ld work? There are already US Bitcoin ETFs, but  they use Bitcoin futures a=
nd so are kind of strange; there are other exchange-traded securities that =
sort of track Bitcoin (Grayscale, MicroStrategy) but they are not particula=
rly close substitutes. This is different: It would just be a pot of Bitcoin=
s, managed by BlackRock, that you could buy into on the stock exchange. It =
is hard to get more institutional and mainstream and respectable these days=
 than BlackRock=E2=80=99s iShares brand.
Also it is fun that Coinbase is the custodian: If you are a US institutiona=
l investor looking for a respectable, trustworthy, upstanding Bitcoin=C2=A0=
company, Coinbase is probably at the top of the list; it is an established =
US public company that has audited financial statements and bothers to thin=
k about compliance. It is also in an existential legal struggle with the SE=
C, which accuses it of operating an unregulated securities exchange for all=
 the=C2=A0other=C2=A0crypto assets it lists. Bitcoin: respectable; other cr=
ypto: apparently not.
Anyway here is BlackRock=E2=80=99s registration statement for this product.=
 There are some delightful risk factors about the state of crypto in 2023; =
here are two fun ones:
Stablecoins are a relatively new phenomenon, and it is impossible to know a=
ll of the risks that they could pose to participants in the bitcoin market.=
 In addition, some have argued that some stablecoins, particularly Tether, =
are improperly issued without sufficient backing in a way that, when the st=
ablecoin is used to pay for bitcoin, could cause artificial rather than gen=
uine demand for bitcoin, artificially inflating the price of bitcoin, and a=
lso argue that those associated with certain stablecoins may be involved in=
 laundering money.
And:
On June 6, 2023, the SEC filed a complaint against the Relevant Coinbase En=
tities in federal district court in the Southern District of New York. =E2=
=80=A6 If the Prime Broker were to be required or choose as a result of a r=
egulatory action (including, for example, the litigation initiated by the S=
EC), to restrict or curtail the services it offers, it could negatively aff=
ect the Trust=E2=80=99s ability to operate or process creations or redempti=
ons of Baskets, which could force the Trust to liquidate or adversely affec=
t the price of the Shares. While the Bitcoin Custodian is not named in the =
complaint, if Coinbase Global, as the parent of the Bitcoin Custodian, is r=
equired, as a result of a judicial determination, or could choose, to restr=
ict or curtail the services its subsidiaries provide to the Trust, or its f=
inancial condition is negatively affected, it could negatively affect the T=
rust=E2=80=99s ability to operate.
=E2=80=9COur custodian might be illegal=E2=80=9D is an unusual risk factor =
for a BlackRock trust, but that=E2=80=99s life in crypto.

      KYC
   =20
 =20

A funny business model for a crypto exchange
  [3]
 would be:
=09Open a crypto exchange that has, and develops a reputation for having, r=
ather lax know-your-customer=C2=A0and anti-money-laundering controls.=09Att=
ract a clientele that is heavily weighted toward customers who could not pa=
ss KYC checks at other, more conservative crypto exchanges.=09Steal all the=
 money.=09File for bankruptcy.=09Announce =E2=80=9Chey good news, we found =
a bidder who will pay back every customer at 100 cents on the dollar.=E2=80=
=9D (The bidder is you, in a fake mustache, using the money you stole.)=09S=
ay to customers =E2=80=9Cokay great, so, all we need is for you to fill out=
 these forms and pass standard KYC checks, and we=E2=80=99ll send the money=
 right over.=E2=80=9D=09Have an extremely strict KYC process for paying bac=
k the customers.=09Keep whatever you don=E2=80=99t give back.

There are various problems here. (For one thing, Steps 1 and 2 could get yo=
u in very bad trouble with the authorities even before you get to Step 3. F=
or another thing, this plan entails stealing from people who can=E2=80=99t =
pass KYC checks, and some=C2=A0people can=E2=80=99t pass KYC checks because=
 they are very bad people indeed, and do you really want to steal from them=
?) Also I do not think that FTX Trading Ltd. followed this plan, because (1=
) it wasn=E2=80=99t known for being=C2=A0particularly lax about KYC and (2)=
 it=E2=80=99s not like anyone is going around saying that FTX is going to p=
ay anyone back at 100 cents on the dollar or anything like that.
Still here is  a funny tweet:


   =20

If you=C2=A0could=C2=A0pass KYC checks to open an account at FTX and send i=
n your money, but you=C2=A0can=E2=80=99t=C2=A0pass the KYC checks to get (w=
hat=E2=80=99s left of) your money back, is that a problem with old FTX or n=
ew FTX?

   =20

=E2=80=9CDon=E2=80=99t put it in email,=E2=80=9D I say around here, as well=

ph from a  US federal criminal indictment=C2=A0(via  Intelligencer) that ob=

Between on or about September 3, 2018, through July 12, 2021, JOSHUA TAYLOR=

But, look, I understand that=C2=A0advice=C2=A0doesn=E2=80=99t help. You can=

ld not put evidence of crimes in writing, but when you get to the little me=
mo field in PayPal or Venmo or your checkbook or whatever, and you are buyi=

=C2=A0writing =E2=80=9Cbraiiiiiins.=E2=80=9D I get it! I am typing this adv=
ice, and I have seen the consequences when people fail to follow it, and I =

Pal, I would=C2=A0absolutely=C2=A0type =E2=80=9Cbraiiiiiins=E2=80=9D in the=
 memo field. =E2=80=9CWelp, guess I=E2=80=99m going to prison,=E2=80=9D I=

a memo field, so you can type your little jokes about your crimes, but it d=

Like disappearing messages, but for this very specific niche temptation. I =
don=E2=80=99t know, man. There are just a lot of Venmo/PayPal/etc. memo fie=
lds in federal indictments.

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That trade netted a $2,000 profit, just a frac=

/p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Under questioning from prosecutor Sam Rothschi=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CIt was great to make money,=E2=80=9D =

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Niranjan said one day he got a text from Goel =

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The next day Goel texted him, =E2=80=9CDid you=

him to buy the options in Lumos, he bought $2,000 worth of call options. A =
few days later, the company announced it was being acquired, the stock pric=
e went up, and the trade generated $2,000 in profit.</p>
</blockquote>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And so at the market-microstructure level, it =
is easy to imagine letting an artificial intelligence model loose on the st=
ock market=C2=A0and telling it =E2=80=9Clearn how to trade profitably,=E2=
=80=9D and the model coming back and saying =E2=80=9Cit seems like the stoc=
k market is dominated by simple market-making algorithms that respond to or=
der-book information, and actually the way to trade profitably is to do a l=
ot of spoofing and market manipulation to trick them.=E2=80=9D (This is mor=
e or less a plot point in Andrew Lipstein=E2=80=99s upcoming hedge-fund nov=
el=C2=A0<em><a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/318168=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And at the level of writing fake press release=
s, generative AI is probably better at writing fake press releases (and ill=

ket manipulator is. The <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/=

ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
 Financial Times reports</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">AI-generated news and images could pose a big =
problem for hedge funds and ultrafast proprietary trading firms that use co=
mplex algorithms to comb vast amounts of news and social media for market-m=
oving signals that they can then rapidly trade.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">While their computers have become more adept a=
t sifting out false news stories and social media posts, executives at quan=
titative trading firms warn that machine-generated misinformation is a new =
frontier.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CAI, to be sure, opens the door to all=
 manner of mischief in the information environment, which is becoming harde=
r to manage,=E2=80=9D said Doug Greenig, founder of hedge fund Florin Court=
 Capital, which bets on longer-term trends in alternative markets rather th=
an on very short-term market moves.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Traders=E2=80=99 particular concern is AI=E2=
=80=99s fast-developing ability to produce highly convincing images and sto=
ries, and in huge quantities.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That could provide a huge number of pitfalls f=
or proprietary trading firms and hedge funds that for years have been inves=
ting heavily in algorithms that parse critical information, assess the lang=
uage and sentiment within a source and use that data as a signal to trigger=
 an automated trade. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yin Luo, head of quantitative research at New =
York data group Wolfe Research, predicts =E2=80=9Ca cat-and-mouse game=E2=
=80=9D between parties spreading market-moving fake news and traders hoping=
 to stay one step ahead.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It is funny to imagine an end state here in wh=
ich markets are entirely dominated by computers=C2=A0playing games with eac=
h other that are so complex and unintuitive that no human can understand th=
em, so that a company will announce surprising good news and the stock will=
 go down and everyone will be like =E2=80=9Ceh, bots, what can you do.=E2=
=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Would it be=C2=A0<em>so=C2=A0</em>different fr=
om how financial markets work now? Financial markets aggregate human views =
and preferences in an emergent way, so that it=E2=80=99s not obvious what t=
he views and preferences are just from the price action. =E2=80=9CStocks we=
nt up because bad economic data convinced investors that the Fed would paus=
e its rate hikes=E2=80=9D is not really=C2=A0<em>that=C2=A0</em>much more i=
ntuitive or accurate an explanation than, like, =E2=80=9Cstocks went up bec=
ause some computers tricked some other computers=C2=A0into thinking economi=
c data was good.=E2=80=9D</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One easily imaginable but also kind of weird f=
uture for crypto would be:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Bitcoin, and I guess Ethereum, are re=
spectable and fully domesticated parts of the traditional financial system.=
 Big asset managers, managing money for pension funds or college endowments=
 or insurance companies or your 401(k),=C2=A0buy billions of dollars of Bit=
coin by calling up their sales coverage at Goldman Sachs or JPMorgan, and t=
hen hold all those Bitcoin in standard respectable institutional custody ar=
rangements.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Pretty much every othe=
r cryptocurrency is banned in the US and eventually withers down to nothing=
.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I do think this would be strange? Much of the =
activity by developers and investors and venture capitalists in crypto in r=
ecent years has been about, in some way,=C2=A0<em>improving on=C2=A0</em>th=
e ideas of Bitcoin (and Ethereum); it would be kind of weird for the base l=
ayer of crypto to be legal and mainstream and for everything built on top o=
f it to be illegal. Also my general sense of crypto ethos is that Bitcoin h=
as traditionally been pretty correlated with, like, =E2=80=9Canarchist onli=
ne drug market,=E2=80=9D while more recent crypto projects have been more l=
ike =E2=80=9Cventure capitalists, tech founders and financial companies bui=
lding new financial technology businesses=E2=80=9D; it=E2=80=99s weird that=
 the former would go mainstream and the latter would be banned.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And yet it makes a certain sense: Bitcoin is=
=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>a fundraising tool for building a new tech platform=
, so it=E2=80=99s not a=C2=A0<em>security=C2=A0</em>under US law, so the US=
 Securities and Exchange Commission can=E2=80=99t crack down on it. Everyth=
ing else is in some loose sense =E2=80=9Cquasi-stock in a quasi-company bui=
lding a tech business,=E2=80=9D so selling or trading it risks violating US=
 securities laws.</p>
